chosen: Intercom was heading toward negative growth when the CEO came back after 2 years away. He spent $100M in cash, killed $60M ARR on purpose and moved 80% of R&D into Fin. Today: $400M ARR & doubled growth two years in a row. Three years ago, Intercom was heading toward negative growth. The CEO had stepped away for two years. When he came back, the company was dying quickly. So he systematically destroyed his own company. He replaced everything: • New values • New mission • New brand • New board • New pricing Moved 80% of R&D into a single AI product. Spent $100M in cash reserves when most people thought AI was hype. The result? Intercom doubled their growth rate two years in a row while every competitor in the space declined. They're now on track to be one of the fastest-growing large software companies in the world. They killed $60M in ARR on purpose. Then they went all in: • Shifted their entire customer base to a new pricing model • Reduced pricing on legacy products • Focused monetization on the AI agent Most founders would rather die than voluntarily destroy $60M in revenue. If you won't make these moves, someone else will: • Cannibalize your own revenue • Bet your cash reserves • Fire against new values to remove people who can't adapt Intercom committed corporate suicide and rebuilt from the mantra of All it will take is destroying everything you love. Most SaaS companies right now are doing the opposite: • Protecting what worked • Defending their margins • Adding AI features instead of becoming AI companies Meanwhile, the companies willing to burn it down are pulling away. Here's my takeaway as a bootstrapped founder: We don't have $100M in cash reserves to bet or 30,000 customers to migrate. But we have something Intercom didn't have three years ago. We have nothing left to protect. That's the advantage that matters right now.
rejected: In 2026, GTM teams will split into two groups: Teams chasing shiny AI tools Teams building REAL growth systems. Guess which ones will win. The last 12 months were chaos for GTM teams. • New AI tools every week • New growth hacks every day • Endless “automations” everyone should try So teams panic. "We're not using this AI tool yet." "We should test this outbound workflow." "I heard this growth hack generates leads." Shiny object syndrome everywhere. But the teams that win in 2026 will focus on two things. 1. Strong GTM foundations Not sexy. But this drives revenue. • Clear ICP • Sharp positioning • Messaging that actually resonates • Offers built from real market research • A brand narrative that connects everything Most companies skip this part. Then wonder why their campaigns don’t work. 2. Systems that compound Not random tactics. Real GTM systems. • Intent signals • Warm outbound • Content repurposing engines • Personal brands for distribution • Sales + content alignment AI helps here. But AI is not the strategy. It’s just the workflow layer. The companies that win in 2026 will still test new tools. But they won’t lose focus. Because the real advantage isn't hacks. It’s clarity + systems. That’s also the thinking behind what we’re building at Content Path. Helping teams turn ideas into scalable content systems. Simple rule for 2026 GTM: Foundations → Systems → Tactics Not the other way around. If you're building GTM in 2026: Focus less on tactics. Focus more on infrastructure. What’s one GTM priority for 2026 you’d add?

chosen: When we started Ground, I didn’t begin with a product. I started with people. I reached out to the CEOs, Heads of Growth, and operators behind the brands I deeply respected - Brands like Equinox, Glossier, Netflix, NHL, to emerging ones like Adapt Naturals, and ROZ. the ones whose missions I believed in. I wanted to understand how they thought about growth, where they were constrained, and what they wished existed. That became Ground. Today, Ground is an autonomous AI revenue system of agents adding ~10% more topline revenue for the brands we work with - these agents operate continuously, inside their existing tools and systems, understanding buying signals and converting intent into revenue. But the part I didn’t expect: In the last 7 days, three customers - some current, some former (!) - reached back out… Not because of a campaign. Not because we chased them. Because they felt the absence and wanted more. One told me: “I have 40,000+ emails in my inbox, but I still remember yours from 2024. You actually understood what we were going through. You made me believe this was real before I fully did.” I told them to stop because I was on the verge of tears, haha. While we’ve always been building an AI company, what mattered early on wasn’t just the technology - it was how we showed up. We educated. We listened. We cared enough to be early - and more importantly, to be patient (even though I’m still working on this one). Inside Ground, I always ask the team to keep a “wish list.” Which companies do you want to see win? Which operators deserve to get promoted? Whose mission actually matters? Because if you build with that level of intentionality, the product compounds in a different way. Today, Ground has driven millions in revenue for brands - often after a ~15 minute onboarding. But what’s more important is this: We’re not just creating artificial intelligence or training agents. We’re building the system that helps the right businesses reach their potential.
rejected: 👋🏼 Founders - it's time to add the 'AI Survival Slide' to your pitch decks. It's what every investor is thinking to themselves anyway so make sure to include some version of it. This week on a long flight without wifi, I reviewed 50+ startup decks that all included the standard slides: problem, solution, team, market etc. But as I flipped through them, I realized that I was waiting for a slide that never came. Right now, how a startup is going to survive AI (and thrive) is top of mind but it's still missing from most pitches. In 2026, the "Competitive Landscape" slide with the checkmarks is no longer enough. Investors don't just want to know how you're better than your current rivals, but why you won't be steamrolled by the next LLM update or a big tech giant adding your core feature to their suite. Don't get me wrong, many startups have good answers to this concern but are not specifically addressing it in their decks. This feels like a mistake in today's uncertain market. Especially if you are building in the b2b / enterprise space, your deck needs to address defensibility through a new lens that may include: 🎯 Deep workflows, not just widgets. Features are easy to replicate but deep, messy, and sticky integrations into a company’s daily operations are not. If your AI is a sidecar chatbot, you’re at risk. If it’s the engine that runs a complex internal process, you’re building a moat. 🎯 Proprietary data loops. Investors are looking for how you are capturing unique private data, the kind of specialized industry intel that isn't available for training public models. That data loop is your fortress. 🎯 The trust layer. In regulated industries like health, finance, or even legal, human-in-the-loop and brand can be a moat. AI can generate the work, but humans still need to sign off on it. How does your product own that trust? Most of what I listed above is already top of mind for the best founders but I'm not seeing it come thru in the actual pitch decks. Don't wait for the investor to ask....Why will you survive? Make it crystal clear and part of the pitch itself.
